title: Government subsidized junk food is making america sicker than ever with Shawn Stevenson

chunks.title: Introduction to American Diet
chunks.summary: The transcript opens with a discussion about the alarming statistic that 70% of the average child's diet in the United States consists of ultra-processed foods. The host introduces Sean Stevenson, who has insights on the American health system and shares thoughts from his new cookbook, emphasizing the decline in community around food and its impact on health.
chunks.transcript: 70% of the average child's diet in the United States is made of ultr processed foods on this week's podcast I had the very distinct pleasure of having my friend Sean Stevenson on to talk about the State of Affairs of the American Health System and the American diet he just wrote a new cookbook which I'm really excited about and as we talk about in this podcast the beginning part of the cookbook is like a little mini book that has so much incredible information his premise is that our health has gone downhill rapidly in America and this is due mostly to eating tons of ultra processed foods and loss of community around eating food and sharing the intentions with which we choose our Foods so this podcast was really fun we talked about a lot of high level stuff that I haven't really thought about before Sean opened my eyes to some of this stuff and taught me some things so I think you guys will enjoy this podcast with Sean Stevenson he's an OG in the health space and I hope you'll check out his new cookbook as well Sean Stevenson in the house thanks for coming

chunks.title: The Impact of Ultra-Processed Foods
chunks.summary: Sean highlights research indicating that over 60% of American adults' calories come from ultra-processed foods, raising concerns about the cultural factors influencing dietary choices. He explains the distinction between processed and ultra-processed foods, focusing on the loss of nutritional value and its implications for health.

chunks.title: Effects of Eating Together
chunks.summary: The conversation turns to the importance of social connections and communal meals. Research indicates that families who eat together tend to consume more essential nutrients and lower amounts of ultra-processed foods, leading to better health outcomes for children.

chunks.title: Cultural Influences on Food Choices
chunks.summary: The hosts delve into how cultural beliefs and the convenience of modern food sourcing affect eating habits and physical activity. They discuss the responsibility of creating a positive food culture within families to foster healthier eating behavior.

chunks.title: Agribusiness and Food System Challenges
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts to the role of agribusiness in the American food system, particularly the reliance on government subsidies for crops like corn and soy. The hosts explore how this affects the affordability and availability of healthier food options.

chunks.title: Changing Perceptions of Nutrition
chunks.summary: Sean talks about misconceptions surrounding nutrition education in medical schools and the influence of pharmaceutical ties on dietary guidelines. He stresses the need for a shift in understanding the foundational role of food in overall health.
chunks.transcript: McDonald's and I was in that Guild for many years but a revolution had to happen my P my pain had to become so bad that I decided to change I had to have this kind of internal revelation after seeing my various Physicians and them telling me that I had this so-called incur able condition and I was just this is just the way my life was going to be I had to I had to have this Revelation that it doesn't have to be this way and they're literally telling me that they can't help me so let me figure out something so that I can just have better days and then I got to the place and my attention I started to to see the things that were in my environment the entire time it's just I wasn't attuned to them I was just attuned to ultra processed foods to dysfunction and sickness because that's what was all I saw in my family and having a family where about 80% of my family members clinically obese that's just all I saw not to mention the heart disease and the cancer and open heart surgeries and the asthma and the list goes on and on and on it was just normalized to me that that's just what it is that's just I'm just going to one of these problems and nothing could be again we've become a culture that believes we can get something for nothing unfortunately and because for years we've been inundated with things that disempower us and reducing our energy reducing our will not willower but our will just to live and I've seen this clinically where people develop this what we label as learned helplessness because they really are trying so hard to take control control chunks.title: Food as Information
chunks.summary: The final segment emphasizes the concept of food as information and its complex nutritional value, arguing against relying on supplements alone. Sean encourages a return to whole foods as pivotal for health and wellness.

chunks.title: Conclusion and Call to Action
chunks.summary: The hosts conclude by reiterating the importance of reconnecting through food, advocating for families to prioritize shared meals. They share strategies for making healthier eating enjoyable and accessible, underscoring the potential for cultural change from within.
